statement: Iceland's labour productivity (GDP per hour worked) is approximately 10–15% below the EU-15 average and 20–25% below Denmark and Norway. In 2024, Iceland's GDP per hour worked was approximately €52 (PPP-adjusted), compared to Denmark's €68, Norway's €75, and the EU-27 average of €42. However, Iceland's total hours worked per person are among the highest in Europe — approximately 1,650 hours per year versus 1,450 for Denmark and 1,380 for Norway. This means Iceland achieves high GDP per capita partly through working longer hours rather than higher productivity per hour.
source_name: OECD — Compendium of Productivity Indicators 2025; Eurostat — Labour productivity per hour worked
caveats: PPP-adjusted productivity comparisons are sensitive to methodology. Iceland's sectoral composition (large fisheries and tourism sectors) naturally produces lower productivity per hour than service-dominated economies. Small sample sizes affect accuracy of hours worked estimates.

related_entries.statement: Iceland has among the highest average wages in Europe. Adjusted for purchasing power parity, average gross wages in Iceland are approximately 15–20% above the EU-27 average and comparable to Denmark and Luxembourg. The statutory minimum effective wage, set through collective agreements rather than legislation, was approximately ISK 425,000 per month (~EUR 2,800) in early 2026. However, the high cost of living — particularly housing, food, and services — erodes much of the wage premium. Real wage growth has been modest since 2020 due to persistent inflation outpacing nominal wage increases.

Iceland consistently has one of the lowest unemployment rates in Europe. The unemployment rate averaged approximately 3.5% over 2015–2025, compared to the EU-27 average of approximately 6.5% over the same period. Even during the 2008 crisis, Iceland's unemployment peaked at around 8% (early 2010) and recovered to pre-crisis levels within five years — faster than most EU member states. Youth unemployment (15–24) in Iceland is typically 7–9%, compared to the EU-27 average of approximately 14% and rates exceeding 25% in southern European countries.
